title: Psychology-Driven Enhancement of Humour Translation
research_questions: Can a Chain-of-Thought Humour Decomposition Mechanism improve humour translation quality by reducing humour loss and linguistic interference?
Does HDM work well on other datasets?
Does HDM work well on other language?
Does HDM work well on open-source models?
How do Humour Theory and the Humour Decomposition Mechanism affect the results in ablation?
Is zero-shot HDM robust to different humour translation prompts?

datasets_used.name: Short Jokes Dataset
datasets_used.size: 500 samples randomly selected for experiments; 100 samples randomly selected for ablation/generality analyses

methods_proposed.name: Humour Decomposition Mechanism (HDM)
methods_proposed.summary: HDM is a three-step Chain-of-Thought prompting paradigm for humour translation: first mine intrinsic knowledge or analysis of the source joke, then translate that analysis into the target language, and finally compose a new target-language joke from the translated analysis.

methods_proposed.name: Humour-theory-enhanced HDM
methods_proposed.summary: The paper integrates a humour theory prompt inspired by Toplyn’s joke-generation structure, requiring the model to decompose each joke into topic, angle, and punchline before translation and recomposition.

humor_theories.detail: Toplyn’s monologue joke generation theory is used to define joke structure as topic, angle, and punchline; these fields are explicitly requested in the Humour Decomposition prompt.
The related work states that incongruity theory views humour as arising from incongruity between readers’ expectations and the ending of a story.

evaluation_methods.detail: GEMBA is used as a GPT4-based no-reference evaluator for humour, fluency, and coherence, with prompts modified for humour translation evaluation.

GEMBA-SQM scores scalar quality from 0 to 100, and GEMBA-STARS scores one-to-five star rankings; reported as SQM-H, SQM-F, SQM-C and STAR-H, STAR-F, STAR-C. Results are averaged over three runs, with invalid responses omitted.

headline_results.finding: The paper reports average gains for HDM of 7.75% in humour, 2.81% in fluency, and 6.13% in coherence for generated translated text.
headline_results.value: 7.75% in humour; 2.81% in fluency; 6.13% in coherence

headline_results.finding: On English-to-Chinese Short Jokes, GPT4-Turbo + HDM achieved the best Table 1 scores across all reported humour, fluency, and coherence metrics.
headline_results.value: SQM-H 70.54; STAR-H 3.45; SQM-F 99.45; STAR-F 4.99; SQM-C 97.73; STAR-C 4.96

headline_results.finding: For GPT4-Turbo on English-to-Chinese, HDM beat MAPS by +11.20 SQM-H, +4.33 SQM-F, and +9.11 SQM-C.
On other datasets, HDM improved over the MAPS baseline across all LLMs and metrics, with improvements of at least 1.84% in humour, 1.7% in fluency, and 2.15% in coherence.
headline_results.value: at least 1.84% in humour; 1.7% in fluency; 2.15% in coherence

headline_results.finding: On English-to-German with Yi-Large, the paper reports HDM improvements of 2.75% in humour, 3.25% in fluency, and 3% in coherence.
headline_results.value: 2.75% in humour; 3.25% in fluency; 3% in coherence

headline_results.finding: In open-source model tests, Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct improved under HDM, while Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct lost humour score.
headline_results.value: Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct: SQM-H 51.60 to 61.80, SQM-F 91.40 to 97.40, SQM-C 86.00 to 91.35; Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct SQM-H 39.80 to 28.70

key_findings: HDM outperforms direct LLM translation, DUAL-REFLECT, and MAPS on Table 1 for all four closed-source backbone models across humour, fluency, and coherence metrics.
The strongest closed-source result is GPT4-Turbo + HDM, which reaches SQM-H 70.54, SQM-F 99.45, and SQM-C 97.73 on English-to-Chinese Short Jokes.
HDM generalizes beyond the main Short Jokes setup to Question-Answer Jokes and SemEval-2021 samples, where it improves every reported model/metric pair over the MAPS baseline.

The method also improves English-to-Spanish and English-to-German translation in nearly all reported settings, although Yi-Large EN→SP fluency is slightly lower with HDM than the baseline: 96.30 vs 96.50.
Ablation shows that HDM is the main contributor, and the topic-angle-punchline humour theory component works better when combined with HDM than when used without HDM.
Open-source models show mixed behavior: larger Qwen2.5 models improve, but smaller models can decline, especially on SQM-H.
limitations: The study is limited to four languages: Chinese, English, Spanish, and German.
All datasets selected in the study are in English, and future work is needed for additional translation directions.
The evaluation lacks human evaluation, even though the paper states human evaluation remains the gold standard for natural language generation quality.
The paper relies on GPT4-based GEMBA automatic metrics, which may introduce bias into the final results.
The applicability of HDM to different types of humour has not been systematically evaluated.
HDM struggles with puns that depend on pronunciation, character shape, and cultural or linguistic elements, such as the “Fleece Navidad” example.
For smaller open-source models, the paper observes a significant decline in evaluation scores, especially SQM-H.

judges.name: GEMBA using a GPT4-based evaluator (GEMBA-SQM and GEMBA-STARS)
judges.detail: All reported quantitative results are automatic GEMBA evaluations of humour, fluency, and coherence. The paper says GEMBA-SQM scores 0–100 and GEMBA-STARS gives 1–5 star rankings; prompts were modified for humour translation; results were averaged over three runs and invalid LLM responses were omitted.

The authors provide qualitative case/error analysis, including examples they describe as correct HDM cases and a pun/cultural-linguistic error case. No formal human annotation protocol or blinding is reported.

generation_selection.scope: Main English-to-Chinese Short Jokes experiments in Table 1 using Gemini1.5-Pro, Yi-Large, GPT3.5-Turbo, and GPT4-Turbo with direct, DUAL-REFLECT, MAPS, and HDM methods
generation_selection.detail: The paper does not report whether outputs were single samples or selected from multiple candidates. No best-of-N generation or selection policy is described. Repeated GEMBA evaluation over three runs is evaluation repetition, not generation selection.

generation_selection.scope: Generality analyses in Tables 2 and 3 on other datasets and target languages
generation_selection.detail: The paper reports MAPS as baseline and HDM as the proposed method, with 100 randomly selected samples per dataset/language due to budget constraints, but does not report candidate counts or any output-selection procedure.

generation_selection.scope: Open-source model generality analysis in Table 4
generation_selection.detail: The paper evaluates Marco-o1-7B, Llama3.1-8B-Instruct, and Qwen2.5 variants with base and HDM settings, but does not report whether multiple generations were sampled or selected.

generation_selection.scope: Prompt-selection analysis in Figure 3 using GPT4-Turbo and four HDM prompt variants
generation_selection.detail: The paper compares four prompts marked V1–V4, but does not report candidate counts, exact selection procedures, or whether outputs were regenerated multiple times.

generation_selection.scope: Ablation study in Table 5 using GPT4-Turbo and Gemini1.5-Pro
generation_selection.detail: The paper reports settings with HDM/HT removed or retained on 100 random samples, but does not report whether outputs were single generations or selected from a candidate pool.

model_provenance.name: Gemini1.5-Pro
model_provenance.detail: Selected as a backbone model; the paper says default hyperparameters were used in the models' playgrounds. No API snapshot or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: Yi-Large
model_provenance.detail: Selected as a backbone model; the paper says default hyperparameters were used in the models' playgrounds. No API snapshot or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: GPT3.5-Turbo
model_provenance.detail: Selected as a backbone model; the paper says default hyperparameters were used in the models' playgrounds. No exact OpenAI model snapshot or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: GPT4-Turbo
model_provenance.detail: Selected as a backbone model and used in prompt-selection and ablation analyses. No exact OpenAI model snapshot or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: Marco-o1-7B
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated in the open-source model analysis. The paper does not report runtime environment, checkpoint hash, or inference date.

model_provenance.name: Llama3.1-8B-Instruct
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated in the open-source model analysis. The table names Llama3.1-8B-Instruct, while the footnote links to Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct; no checkpoint hash, runtime environment, or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated in the open-source model analysis. No checkpoint hash, runtime environment, or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated in the open-source model analysis. No checkpoint hash, runtime environment, or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated in the open-source model analysis. No checkpoint hash, runtime environment, or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: GPT4 used through GEMBA
model_provenance.detail: The evaluator is described only as a GPT4-based GEMBA metric. The exact GPT-4 variant, API snapshot, inference date, and access route are not reported.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: 3 sequential LLM generation steps per input joke are specified: humour decomposition, translation of the analysis, and humour composition into the target-language joke.
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: For the four closed-source backbone models, the paper states that default hyperparameters in their playgrounds were used; exact parameters are not reported. Sampling settings for open-source models are not reported.
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: Budget constraints are mentioned as the reason for sample sizes, but no compute or monetary cost is reported.
inference_budgets.detail: The method prompts the same backbone LLM to analyze the joke, translate the analysis, and generate the final target-language joke. The paper does not state max tokens, temperature, top-p, seeds, or whether decoding was deterministic.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: Not fully reported; HDM call structure is specified, but direct translation, DUAL-REFLECT, and MAPS call counts/implementation details are not stated in this paper.
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Default playground hyperparameters for Gemini1.5-Pro, Yi-Large, GPT3.5-Turbo, and GPT4-Turbo.
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: 500 Short Jokes Dataset samples were randomly selected due to budget constraints; no cost or compute is reported.
inference_budgets.detail: The main experiment translates English-to-Chinese and evaluates direct backbone outputs plus DUAL-REFLECT, MAPS, and HDM with GEMBA metrics.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: Not fully reported; HDM uses its three-step procedure, but baseline and open-source-model call counts are not reported.
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: The paper states that 100 samples were randomly selected in each dataset and language due to budget constraints; no compute or cost is reported.
inference_budgets.detail: The analyses cover Question-Answer Jokes and SemEval-2021 for English-to-Chinese, English-to-Spanish and English-to-German translation, and open-source models on Short Jokes.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: Not fully reported; settings remove HDM and/or humour theory, but exact prompt-call counts for each ablation setting are not specified.
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: 100 samples were randomly selected for the ablation study; no cost or compute is reported.
inference_budgets.detail: The ablation compares full method, -HDM, -HT, and base for GPT4-Turbo and Gemini1.5-Pro on English-to-Chinese and English-to-Spanish Short Jokes.
The paper compares four HDM prompts using GPT4-Turbo, but does not report the exact prompts in the text, sample size for this figure, number of calls, or decoding settings.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: The paper reports averaging GEMBA results over three runs in each type of experiment, but does not report exact LLM calls per output or per metric.
inference_budgets.detail: GEMBA-SQM and GEMBA-STARS are used for humour, fluency, and coherence. Invalid responses outside the expected score/ranking ranges are omitted, but their frequency is not reported.

human_baselines.description: No human evaluation or human translation baseline is included. The paper explicitly notes the absence of human evaluation as a limitation and says future work will incorporate human judgments.
human_baselines.detail: All quantitative comparisons are between LLM-based translation methods and are evaluated by GPT4-based GEMBA metrics.

contamination_risk.basis: The experiments use public/open-source humour datasets, including a Kaggle Short Jokes Dataset, Kaggle Question-Answer Jokes, and SemEval-2021 Task 7 samples. The paper provides no decontamination analysis, private held-out items, training-data overlap checks, or inference dates. Randomly selecting 500 or 100 samples addresses budget but is not a contamination control. Because public jokes may have appeared in training data for later proprietary or open models, contamination risk is at least moderate, although the task is no-reference translation/generation rather than exact-label prediction.

llm_judge_dependence.affected_claims: All headline claims about HDM improving humour, fluency, and coherence; generalization to other datasets/languages/models; prompt robustness; and ablation effects are based on GPT4-based GEMBA judgments.
llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: None. The paper states that human evaluation is absent and remains future work.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: GEMBA is the sole quantitative evaluator. The paper cites prior work suggesting GPT-4 opinions may overlap with human reviewers, but it does not validate GEMBA scores against human judgments in this study.

reporting_gaps: No human participants, annotators, or human baseline translations are reported.
Exact GEMBA evaluator version/snapshot, API/access route, inference date, prompts, and decoding parameters are not reported.
Exact API snapshots or model versions beyond model aliases are not reported for GPT3.5-Turbo, GPT4-Turbo, Gemini1.5-Pro, or Yi-Large.
Inference dates are not reported for any generation or evaluation model.
Runtime environment, checkpoint hashes, hardware, and serving setup are not reported for the open-source models.
The paper states default playground hyperparameters for closed-source models but does not report temperature, top-p, max tokens, stop sequences, seeds, or determinism.
The number of generated candidates per item and any output-selection policy are not reported; no best-of-N procedure is described.
Implementation details and call budgets for DUAL-REFLECT and MAPS baselines are not reported in this paper.
Random subset sample IDs and random seeds for selecting the 500- and 100-sample subsets are not reported.
Invalid GEMBA response counts/rates and how omissions affected denominators are not reported.
Prompt-selection analysis does not report the exact V1–V4 prompt texts or clearly state the sample size and inference settings for Figure 3.
No contamination or memorization analysis is reported for public joke datasets.
No statistical significance tests, confidence intervals, or variance estimates beyond averaging over three GEMBA runs are reported.
